passages.explanation: Seeing the crowds, Jesus goes up a mountain, sits down — the posture of a rabbi about to teach — and his disciples come to him. He opens with the Beatitudes: blessed are the poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, the merciful, the pure in heart, the peacemakers, and those persecuted for righteousness' sake. Each blessing inverts the world's value system. The kingdom doesn't belong to the powerful, the comfortable, or the self-assured — it belongs to those who know they need it. Jesus is describing the character of kingdom citizens, not giving a self-help checklist.

You are the salt of the earth, Jesus tells his disciples. But if salt loses its flavour, what can make it salty again? It's good for nothing but to be thrown out and trampled. You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill can't be hidden. No one lights a lamp and puts it under a basket — they put it on a stand so it gives light to everyone in the house. Let your light shine before people so they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven. Salt preserves and light reveals. Both are useless if they don't do what they're made for.

Jesus anticipates the objection: don't think I came to destroy the law or the prophets. I didn't come to destroy but to fulfil. Until heaven and earth pass away, not even the smallest letter or stroke will pass from the law until all is accomplished. Whoever breaks one of the least of these commandments and teaches others to do so will be called least in the Kingdom of Heaven. Unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you will never enter the Kingdom of Heaven. This is the interpretive key to the whole sermon. Jesus isn't abolishing the law — he's taking it to its fullest meaning. And that bar is higher than anyone expected.

You have heard it was said to the ancient ones: you shall not murder. But I tell you that everyone who is angry with his brother without a cause will be in danger of the judgement. Whoever says to his brother, Raca — worthless — will be in danger of the council. Whoever says, you fool, will be in danger of the fire of Gehenna. If you're offering your gift at the altar and remember that your brother has something against you, leave your gift, go be reconciled first, then come and offer. Agree with your adversary quickly. Jesus pushes the commandment past the act to the attitude. Murder begins in contempt.

You have heard it was said: you shall not commit adultery. But I tell you that everyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart. If your right eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out. If your right hand causes you to stumble, cut it off. It's better to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to be thrown into Gehenna. The language is deliberately extreme — Jesus isn't recommending self-mutilation. He's saying: deal with sin at the root with the same urgency you'd use if your life depended on it. Because it does.

It was also said: whoever divorces his wife, let him give her a certificate of divorce. But I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for the cause of sexual immorality, makes her an adulteress; and whoever marries her when she is divorced commits adultery. In the ancient world, divorce was easy for men and devastating for women. Jesus tightens the standard, not to be harsh, but to protect the vulnerable. The certificate of divorce was a concession, not a right.

Again, you have heard it was said: you shall not make false vows, but shall perform your vows to the Lord. But I tell you, don't swear at all — not by heaven, not by earth, not by Jerusalem, not by your own head. Let your yes be yes and your no be no. Anything more comes from the evil one. The elaborate system of oath-taking had become a way of grading honesty — some vows were binding, others weren't. Jesus cuts through it: just be a person whose word means something.

You have heard it was said: an eye for an eye and a tooth for a tooth. But I tell you, don't resist the one who is evil. If someone strikes you on your right cheek, turn the other also. If someone wants to sue you and take your coat, give your cloak as well. If someone forces you to go one mile, go two. Give to the one who asks, and don't turn away from one who wants to borrow. Eye for eye was originally a limit on retaliation — no more than equal. Jesus goes further: absorb the wrong rather than perpetuate the cycle.

You have heard it was said: love your neighbour and hate your enemy. But I tell you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who mistreat and persecute you — so that you may be children of your Father in heaven. He makes his sun rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the just and the unjust. If you love only those who love you, what reward do you have? Even the tax collectors do that. Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect. The standard isn't reciprocity — it's the character of God. Love your enemies isn't a suggestion; it's the family resemblance.
